nativeRange.habitat: Rocky bottoms in clear water, often near crevices and small caves at depths up to 30 m; occasionally in kelp forests

diet.notes: In the wild, the Garibaldi feeds mainly on attached invertebrates such as sponges, polychaete worms, anemones, and red algae. In aquariums, it readily accepts a variety of meaty foods including brine shrimp and mysis shrimp, as well as herbivore preparations, flake foods, and pellets. Feeding twice daily is generally recommended.

Product hero image from LiveAquaria listing. High-resolution (width=1945), clean composition with no visible watermark. Recommended only if Wikimedia image is insufficient.

summary: The Garibaldi Damsel (Hypsypops rubicundus) is the official state marine fish of California, recognized for its brilliant orange coloration. It requires cooler water than most saltwater species and a large aquarium, and is widely considered extremely aggressive toward its own kind — generally kept one per tank. Can live 17 to 25 years in captivity.
careNotes: The Garibaldi Damsel is the official state marine fish of California, prized for its vivid orange coloration. Juveniles display distinctive electric-blue spots and blue-outlined fins that fade with age. This species requires cooler water than most tropical marines — temperatures of 18–23°C (64–74°F) are recommended for aquarium care, and standard reef temperatures above 26°C may stress the fish. Only one individual should be kept per tank, as the species is widely considered extremely aggressive toward its own kind. It can be housed with large, robust semi-aggressive species such as large angelfishes, tangs, parrotfish, wrasses, and groupers, but small or passive tankmates should be avoided. Males are sexually dimorphic, distinguished by a larger body and a distinctive fleshy frontal lobe. Wild specimens have been recorded reaching 35 cm in total length and living up to 57 years; aquarium-kept individuals typically remain closer to 30 cm and live 17–25 years. Note that wild habitat temperatures in the species' natural range have been measured as low as 15.3–19.2°C — somewhat cooler than the 18–23°C range generally recommended for aquarium care.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been achieved. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 5–6 years of age, when the fish is around 20–23 cm (8–9 inches). Spawning in the wild runs from March to July. Males construct and guard algae-based nests and can lay 15,000–80,000 eggs per clutch, with an incubation period of 2–3 weeks. Males are known to aggressively defend nest sites and have been observed biting divers who approach too closely. The species' slow maturation and complex breeding behavior make captive reproduction impractical with current methods.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (30 cm SL max), temperature range, native distribution, family (Pomacentridae), and order. FishBase wild habitat temperature (15.3–19.2°C) is cooler than aquarium-care recommendations; aquarium range used for this file.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, temperament, tank requirements, and breeding info. Minor disagreements: temperature range (20–26°C vs LiveAquaria 17.8–23.3°C) and minimum tank size (100 gal vs LiveAquaria 180 gal) — conservative LiveAquaria figures used. FKW incorrectly listed family as Pomacanthidae; Pomacentridae (per FishBase) is correct.

fish.breedingNotes: Not successfully bred in captivity. Males reach sexual maturity at around 5 to 6 years of age. Wild spawning takes place from March through July, with males constructing and guarding algae-based nests.
